behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is module handoff: CSV parsing/row indexing + schema validation + DLQ write path. A unit test of only the validator could pass while row-number propagation to DLQ is broken. Rejected System because the behavior text does not require real OS/network/subprocess behavior; an in-process pipeline wiring test with fake input and fake DLQ sink can catch this real failure.
behaviors.test_description: Run the row-processing pipeline in-process with a small CSV fixture containing missing required fields. Assert invalid rows are routed to DLQ records and each DLQ entry preserves the original CSV row number.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: This behavior targets deterministic transformation logic in one function/object boundary (date parsing/normalization). No module handoff, infra, or external dependency is required to reproduce failures. Rejected Integration because behavior text is about conversion correctness itself, not cross-module state transfer.
behaviors.test_description: Table-driven unit test for the date-normalization function: inputs across ISO 8601, US, and European formats, plus edge cases, asserting exact canonical output.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: This is a resource/performance failure boundary tied to real runtime behavior (memory pressure, timeout clocks, streaming/buffering effects). In-process mocks can hide OOM and timing regressions. Requires real process execution and OS-observed memory/time behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the pipeline as a real process against a 500k-row fixture, with production-like timeout/memory settings. Assert completion before timeout and memory stays below threshold (no OOM/kill).

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in retry orchestration between pipeline code and S3 client abstraction. A deterministic stub/fake S3 endpoint returning 503 then success can expose backoff/retry bugs without requiring real AWS network calls. Rejected System because behavior text focuses on retry policy correctness, not real network conditions (latency/jitter/TLS/socket errors).
behaviors.test_description: Wire pipeline with a fake S3 client/server that returns 503 for first N attempts and then success. Assert exponential backoff intervals and that batch continues instead of terminal failure.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: This boundary includes restart semantics plus durable DB state across process lifecycle. Real failure often appears only when a process is interrupted/restarted and checkpoint/idempotency logic replays writes. Requires real Postgres + real restart behavior (subprocess/OS), not just in-memory module wiring.
behaviors.test_description: Start pipeline against real Postgres with a batch fixture; interrupt after partial progress, restart pipeline, then assert final table has exactly-once rows (no duplicates) and expected total count.
